mission.title: Install llama.cpp Server on Kubernetes
mission.description: llama.cpp is the lightweight, dependency-minimal GGUF inference runtime from ggml-org. It ships a small HTTP server (`llama-server`) that speaks the OpenAI chat-completions API at `/v1/chat/completions`, so it drops in wherever an OpenAI-compatible endpoint is expected. This mission deploys `llama-server` as a Kubernetes Deployment using the official `ghcr.io/ggml-org/llama.cpp:server` image, mounts a PersistentVolumeClaim for the GGUF model cache, and exposes it through a ClusterIP Service. Both CPU and GPU variants of the image are covered. Upstream docs: https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/master/tools/server/README.md .

steps.title: Decide CPU vs GPU image
steps.description:
llama.cpp publishes two server image flavors on GHCR:

- CPU only: `ghcr.io/ggml-org/llama.cpp:server` — runs anywhere, uses AVX/AVX2 when available.
- CUDA: `ghcr.io/ggml-org/llama.cpp:server-cuda` — requires the NVIDIA device plugin on the node and `nvidia.com/gpu` resource requests.

For a first install on a laptop kind cluster, stay on the CPU image. Pick the CUDA image only if you already have a working GPU node with the device plugin installed. Verify with:
```bash
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.capacity.nvidia\.com/gpu}{"\n"}{end}'
```

steps.title: Create the namespace
steps.description:
Keep llama.cpp in its own namespace so the workload, PVC, and Service are easy to clean up later:
```bash
kubectl create namespace llamacpp
```

steps.title: Create a PVC for the GGUF model cache
steps.description:
GGUF model files range from ~2 GB (Phi-3 mini q4) to 40+ GB (Llama 3 70B). Mount a PersistentVolume so the model survives pod restarts and you do not re-download on every rollout. This manifest requests 20 GiB, which holds a typical 7B-class quantized model with room to spare — raise the size for larger models:
```bash
cat <<'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: llamacpp-models
  namespace: llamacpp
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 20Gi
EOF
```

steps.title: Deploy llama-server
steps.description:
This Deployment runs the server image with `--host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080` so the Service can reach it, mounts the PVC at `/models`, and uses an initContainer to pull the model from Hugging Face on first start. Replace `HF_REPO` and `HF_FILE` with the model you want — the example pulls Phi-3 mini 4K Instruct Q4:
```bash
cat <<'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: llama-server
  namespace: llamacpp
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: llama-server
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: llama-server
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: fetch-model
          image: curlimages/curl:8.8.0
          command:
            - sh
            - -c
            - test -f /models/model.gguf || curl -L -o /models/model.gguf https://huggingface.co/microsoft/Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct-gguf/resolve/main/Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct-q4.gguf
          volumeMounts:
            - name: models
              mountPath: /models
      containers:
        - name: server
          image: ghcr.io/ggml-org/llama.cpp:server
          args:
            - --host
            - 0.0.0.0
            - --port
            - "8080"
            - --model
            - /models/model.gguf
            - --ctx-size
            - "4096"
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
              name: http
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "2"
              memory: 6Gi
            limits:
              cpu: "4"
              memory: 8Gi
          volumeMounts:
            - name: models
              mountPath: /models
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 10
      volumes:
        - name: models
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: llamacpp-models
EOF
```
Wait for the Deployment to roll out — the first start takes a few minutes because the initContainer has to download the model file:
```bash
kubectl -n llamacpp rollout status deploy/llama-server --timeout=10m
```

steps.title: Expose llama-server as a Service
steps.description:
A ClusterIP Service keeps the endpoint inside the cluster. Clients on the same cluster reach it at `http://llama-server.llamacpp.svc.cluster.local:8080`:
```bash
cat <<'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: llama-server
  namespace: llamacpp
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: llama-server
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8080
      targetPort: 8080
EOF
```

steps.title: Verify the OpenAI-compatible endpoint
steps.description:
Port-forward to your workstation and hit the chat-completions endpoint to confirm inference works end to end:
```bash
kubectl -n llamacpp port-forward svc/llama-server 8080:8080
```
In a second terminal:
```bash
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1/chat/completions -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Say hi in one word."}]}'
```
A successful response returns a JSON body with a `choices[0].message.content` field.

resolution.summary: A successful llama.cpp install has the `llama-server` Deployment in the `llamacpp` namespace reporting one Ready replica, the PVC bound with the downloaded GGUF file present at `/models/model.gguf`, and `curl http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1/chat/completions` returning a valid OpenAI-compatible response. The Service resolves at `llama-server.llamacpp.svc.cluster.local:8080` for in-cluster clients.

uninstall.title: Delete the Deployment and Service
uninstall.description:
Remove the workload first so nothing is reading from the PVC when it is deleted:
```bash
kubectl -n llamacpp delete deploy/llama-server svc/llama-server --ignore-not-found
```

uninstall.title: Delete the model PVC
uninstall.description:
The PVC holds the downloaded GGUF file — deleting it releases the underlying PersistentVolume (depending on your StorageClass reclaim policy, the PV may be retained or recycled):
```bash
kubectl -n llamacpp delete pvc/llamacpp-models --ignore-not-found
```

uninstall.title: Delete the namespace
uninstall.description:
Finally remove the namespace:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace llamacpp --ignore-not-found
```

upgrade.title: Bump the server image tag
upgrade.description:
llama.cpp publishes GHCR images on every master merge, tagged with the commit short SHA and with rolling tags like `server` and `server-cuda`. For a predictable upgrade path, pin to a specific commit SHA rather than the rolling `server` tag — check https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pkgs/container/llama.cpp for available tags. Edit the Deployment to the new tag:
```bash
kubectl -n llamacpp set image deploy/llama-server server=ghcr.io/ggml-org/llama.cpp:server-<sha>
kubectl -n llamacpp rollout status deploy/llama-server --timeout=5m
```

upgrade.title: Swap the model file
upgrade.description:
To switch to a different model, either edit the initContainer args to pull a different GGUF and delete the existing file, or exec into the pod and `curl` the new file into `/models`. After the new file is in place, restart the Deployment so llama-server loads it:
```bash
kubectl -n llamacpp rollout restart deploy/llama-server
```

troubleshooting.title: initContainer fails to download the model
troubleshooting.description:
If the initContainer errors out, the most common causes are a bad Hugging Face path, a gated model (some HF repos require accepting a license or passing a token), or cluster egress blocking huggingface.co. Check the init logs:
```bash
kubectl -n llamacpp logs -l app=llama-server -c fetch-model --tail=100
```
If the cluster cannot reach huggingface.co, download the GGUF file to your workstation, then `kubectl cp` it into the pod's `/models` directory and remove the initContainer from the Deployment.

troubleshooting.title: llama-server crashloops with OOM
troubleshooting.description:
Large models or long context windows can exhaust the container memory limit. Check the last termination reason:
```bash
kubectl -n llamacpp describe pod -l app=llama-server
```
Raise the memory limit and request in the Deployment, or switch to a smaller quantization (q3_K_M is ~30% smaller than q4_K_M with a modest quality hit), or reduce `--ctx-size`.

troubleshooting.title: Readiness probe failing but server is up
troubleshooting.description: If the pod is running but `kubectl rollout status` times out, the readiness probe may be hitting the server before the model is fully loaded. Inspect the server logs for the `model loaded` line and raise `initialDelaySeconds` on the probe. On a laptop CPU, Phi-3 mini takes ~20-40 seconds to load; 7B-class models can take a minute or more.

troubleshooting.title: GPU image fails to start on CPU nodes
troubleshooting.description: The `server-cuda` image requires the NVIDIA device plugin and a node with `nvidia.com/gpu` resources. On a CPU-only cluster you will see the pod pending with an insufficient-resources event. Switch the image to `ghcr.io/ggml-org/llama.cpp:server` and remove any `nvidia.com/gpu` resource requests.

security.title: No privileged access required
security.description: The `llama-server` container runs as an unprivileged process. It does not need hostPath mounts, hostNetwork, or CAP_SYS_ADMIN. The Deployment in this mission should pass the Kubernetes Pod Security Admission `baseline` profile with no changes; for `restricted`, add a securityContext with `runAsNonRoot: true`, `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`, a `seccompProfile` of `RuntimeDefault`, and drop all Linux capabilities.

security.title: Model provenance
security.description: GGUF files are arbitrary binary blobs loaded into the inference engine. Only download models from sources you trust. Hugging Face repos under well-known publishers (`microsoft/`, `meta-llama/`, `mistralai/`, `TheBloke/`) are the common path. Pin to a specific commit SHA in the HF URL if the upstream repo could rewrite history. For air-gapped installs, mirror the GGUF file to an internal object store and rewrite the initContainer to pull from there instead of huggingface.co.

security.title: Egress surface
security.description: On first start, the initContainer reaches huggingface.co over HTTPS to fetch the GGUF model. After that the workload runs fully offline — `llama-server` makes no outbound calls of its own. If your cluster has a NetworkPolicy-enforced egress deny-by-default posture, open egress to `huggingface.co` only for the initial sync, or pre-populate the PVC out-of-band and remove the initContainer entirely.

security.title: No built-in authentication
security.description: `llama-server` does not authenticate callers out of the box. Keep the Service as ClusterIP and never expose it via Ingress without a gateway in front. For multi-tenant clusters, add an authenticating reverse proxy (oauth2-proxy, kube-rbac-proxy, or an Istio/Envoy AuthorizationPolicy) between the Service and any client namespace. Alternatively, use the `--api-key` flag on `llama-server` to require a shared bearer token (set via an env var sourced from a Kubernetes Secret, not baked into the manifest).

security.title: Relationship to the KubeStellar Console SECURITY-MODEL
security.description: The Console's AI-agent code path is architected so user chat content and command output reach the LLM provider, but kubeconfig files and cluster credentials never do — see docs/security/SECURITY-MODEL.md in the kubestellar/console repo. Pointing a CLI agent (codex, claude, gemini-cli) at a llama.cpp Service keeps inference inside the cluster, which is the strongest local-LLM posture the Console supports today. Chat content still leaves the agent pod, but it no longer leaves the cluster.

prerequisites.description: Any Kubernetes cluster with a default StorageClass that supports ReadWriteOnce volumes. For the CUDA image variant, the cluster must have at least one node exposing `nvidia.com/gpu` via the NVIDIA device plugin. Internet egress to huggingface.co is required on first start so the initContainer can fetch the GGUF model file — in air-gapped clusters, pre-populate the PVC instead.
